question: What is the standard deduction available to salaried individuals in FY 2024-25?
response: A standard deduction of ₹50,000 is available to salaried individuals and pensioners under Section 16(ia) of the Income Tax Act. This deduction is available under both the Old Tax Regime and the New Tax Regime (enhanced to ₹75,000 under New Regime from FY 2024-25 as per Budget 2024).
source: Income Tax Act, Section 16(ia); Finance Act 2023; Union Budget 2024

question: Is standard deduction available under the New Tax Regime in FY 2024-25?
response: Yes. Standard deduction is available under the New Tax Regime. It was initially ₹50,000 and has been enhanced to ₹75,000 for salaried individuals under the New Tax Regime effective FY 2024-25 as per the Union Budget 2024.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 16(ia); Finance Act 2024; Union Budget 2024

question: What is the maximum deduction allowed under Section 80C?
response: The maximum deduction under Section 80C is ₹1,50,000 per financial year. Eligible investments include PPF, ELSS, NSC, 5-year tax-saving FDs, life insurance premiums, principal repayment of home loan, tuition fees, and Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana. Note: Section 80C deductions are not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80C; Finance Act 2023

question: Are PPF contributions eligible under Section 80C?
response: Yes, contributions made to the Public Provident Fund (PPF) qualify for deduction under Section 80C, subject to the overall limit of ₹1,50,000. PPF also enjoys EEE (Exempt-Exempt-Exempt) tax status — contribution, interest, and maturity are all tax-free. This deduction is only available under the Old Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80C; PPF Scheme Rules

question: Are ELSS investments eligible under Section 80C?
response: Yes, investments in Equity Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS) qualify for deduction up to ₹1,50,000 under Section 80C. ELSS has the shortest lock-in period of 3 years among 80C instruments. Returns are subject to LTCG tax under Section 112A. This deduction is only available under the Old Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80C; SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations

question: What is the maximum deduction under Section 80D for health insurance premiums?
response: Under Section 80D, deduction is allowed as follows: (a) Up to ₹25,000 for premium paid for self, spouse, and dependent children; (b) Additional ₹25,000 for parents' health insurance (₹50,000 if parents are senior citizens); (c) If the insured individual is also a senior citizen, the limit is ₹50,000. Maximum combined deduction can go up to ₹1,00,000. This deduction is not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80D; Finance Act 2018

question: What deduction is available under Section 80E for education loan interest?
response: Under Section 80E, the entire amount of interest paid on a loan taken for higher education is deductible with no upper cap. The deduction is available for a maximum of 8 consecutive assessment years starting from the year of commencement of repayment. It covers courses pursued by the taxpayer, spouse, children, or the student for whom the taxpayer is the legal guardian. This deduction is not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80E

question: What is the deduction limit under Section 80TTA?
response: Under Section 80TTA, individuals (other than senior citizens) and HUFs can claim a deduction of up to ₹10,000 on interest earned from savings bank accounts held with a bank, cooperative bank, or post office. Interest from FDs and recurring deposits is not covered. This deduction is not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80TTA

question: What deduction is available to senior citizens under Section 80TTB?
response: Senior citizens (aged 60 years or above) can claim a deduction of up to ₹50,000 under Section 80TTB on interest income from deposits (savings accounts, FDs, and recurring deposits) with banks, cooperative banks, or post offices. Section 80TTA is not applicable to senior citizens; Section 80TTB supersedes it. This deduction is not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80TTB; Finance Act 2018

question: What is the maximum deduction under Section 24(b) for home loan interest on self-occupied property?
response: Under Section 24(b), a deduction of up to ₹2,00,000 per annum is allowed on interest paid on a home loan for a self-occupied property. For a let-out property, the entire interest is deductible without limit (subject to set-off restrictions). The deduction is available only under the Old Tax Regime for self-occupied property; let-out property interest may still be claimed under the New Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 24(b)

question: How is HRA exemption calculated?
response: HRA exemption under Section 10(13A) is the least of the following three: (a) Actual HRA received from employer; (b) Rent paid minus 10% of basic salary + DA; (c) 50% of basic salary + DA for employees living in metro cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata) or 40% for non-metro cities. HRA exemption is not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 10(13A); Rule 2A

question: How is LTCG on listed equity shares taxed in FY 2024-25?
response: Under Section 112A, long-term capital gains (LTCG) from listed equity shares, equity-oriented mutual funds, or units of a business trust are taxed at 10% (without indexation) on gains exceeding ₹1,00,000 in a financial year, provided STT has been paid on acquisition and transfer. Gains up to ₹1,00,000 are exempt. The holding period must exceed 12 months.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 112A; Finance Act 2018; Finance Act 2023

question: What is the tax rate for STCG under Section 111A?
response: Short-term capital gains (STCG) arising from the transfer of listed equity shares, equity-oriented mutual fund units, or units of a business trust where STT has been paid are taxed at 15% under Section 111A (proposed to be raised to 20% from FY 2024-25 per Union Budget 2024). The holding period is 12 months or less.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 111A; Union Budget 2024

question: How is LTCG on gold or debt mutual funds taxed?
response: LTCG on physical gold, gold ETFs, and debt mutual funds is taxed under Section 112. For physical gold and gold ETFs (held for more than 24 months), tax is at 20% with indexation benefit. For debt mutual funds (where STT is not paid), gains are added to income and taxed at slab rates post April 2023 amendment, removing the 20% with indexation benefit.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 112; Finance Act 2023

question: What rebate is available under Section 87A under the New Tax Regime in FY 2024-25?
response: Under the New Tax Regime, a resident individual with total income up to ₹7,00,000 is eligible for a rebate of up to ₹25,000 under Section 87A, effectively making the tax liability nil. Under the Old Tax Regime, the rebate is up to ₹12,500 for income up to ₹5,00,000.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 87A; Finance Act 2023

question: What are the income tax slab rates under the New Tax Regime for FY 2024-25?
source: Income Tax Act, Section 115BAC; Union Budget 2024

question: What are the income tax slab rates under the Old Tax Regime for individuals below 60 years?
response: Under the Old Tax Regime: Nil (up to ₹2,50,000); 5% (₹2,50,001–₹5,00,000); 20% (₹5,00,001–₹10,00,000); 30% (above ₹10,00,000). Applicable surcharge and 4% Health & Education Cess are levied additionally.
source: Income Tax Act, First Schedule; Finance Act 2023

question: What is the rate of Health and Education Cess?
response: Health and Education Cess is levied at 4% on the amount of income tax including surcharge (if any). It replaced the earlier 3% Education Cess from FY 2018-19. It applies uniformly under both Old and New Tax Regimes.
source: Finance Act 2018; Income Tax Act, Section 2(11)

question: At what income levels does surcharge apply to individuals and what are the rates?
response: Surcharge on income tax for individuals: 10% (income > ₹50 lakh to ₹1 crore); 15% (> ₹1 crore to ₹2 crore); 25% (> ₹2 crore to ₹5 crore); 37% (> ₹5 crore) under Old Regime. Under New Tax Regime, the maximum surcharge is capped at 25% (the 37% rate is not applicable), effective from FY 2023-24.
source: Income Tax Act, Surcharge Provisions; Finance Act 2023

question: Who can file ITR-1 (Sahaj)?
response: ITR-1 can be filed by a resident individual (not ordinarily resident or non-resident) whose total income does not exceed ₹50 lakh and comprises: income from salary or pension; income from one house property; income from other sources (excluding winnings from lottery or horse races). Individuals with agricultural income up to ₹5,000 can also use ITR-1. Directors, foreign asset holders, and those with TDS under Section 194N cannot file ITR-1.
source: ITR-1 Instructions; Income Tax Rules, Rule 12

question: Who should file ITR-2?
response: ITR-2 must be filed by individuals and HUFs who do not have income from business or profession but have income from: more than one house property; capital gains; foreign assets or foreign income; agricultural income exceeding ₹5,000; directorships in a company; or income exceeding ₹50 lakh.
source: ITR-2 Instructions; Income Tax Rules, Rule 12

question: Who is required to file ITR-3?
response: ITR-3 must be filed by individuals and HUFs who have income from proprietary business or profession (i.e., income from 'profits and gains of business or profession'). It is also applicable if the person is a partner in a firm. It covers all scenarios that ITR-2 covers, in addition to business/profession income.
source: ITR-3 Instructions; Income Tax Rules, Rule 12

question: Who can file ITR-4 (Sugam)?
response: ITR-4 can be filed by resident individuals, HUFs, and firms (other than LLP) whose total income does not exceed ₹50 lakh and who have opted for presumptive taxation under Section 44AD (business), Section 44ADA (professionals), or Section 44AE (transport business). Those with foreign assets, more than one house property, or capital gains cannot use ITR-4.
source: ITR-4 Instructions; Income Tax Rules, Rule 12

question: What is the presumptive income rate under Section 44AD?
response: Under Section 44AD, eligible businesses (individuals, HUFs, and firms excluding LLPs with turnover up to ₹2 crore) may declare income at 8% of total turnover. For amounts received via digital modes (bank transfer, cheque, RTGS, NEFT, etc.), the presumptive rate is 6%. No further deductions are allowed under Sections 30–38.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 44AD; Finance Act 2016

question: What is the presumptive income rate under Section 44ADA?
response: Under Section 44ADA, eligible professionals (doctors, lawyers, architects, engineers, accountants, etc.) with gross receipts up to ₹50 lakh may declare income at 50% of gross receipts. This was enhanced to ₹75 lakh (if 95%+ receipts are through banking channels) per Finance Act 2023. No books of accounts or audit is required.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 44ADA; Finance Act 2023

question: What is presumptive income under Section 44AE?
response: Under Section 44AE, for goods carriage owners with up to 10 vehicles, presumptive income is ₹1,000 per ton per month for heavy goods vehicles, and ₹7,500 per vehicle per month for other vehicles. The taxpayer cannot own more than 10 goods vehicles at any time during the year to be eligible.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 44AE

question: What deduction is allowed under Section 80G for donations?
response: Under Section 80G, donations to approved funds, institutions, or charitable organizations are eligible for deduction. The deduction is either 100% or 50% of the donated amount. Certain donations (like PM Relief Fund) qualify for 100% deduction without a qualifying limit, while others are subject to a qualifying limit of 10% of adjusted gross total income. Donations above ₹2,000 must be made via non-cash modes.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80G; Finance Act 2017

question: Is the maturity amount of a life insurance policy taxable?
response: Maturity proceeds of a life insurance policy are exempt under Section 10(10D) provided the annual premium does not exceed 10% of the sum assured (for policies issued on or after 1 April 2012). For ULIPs issued after 1 February 2021, if the aggregate annual premium exceeds ₹2.5 lakh, gains are taxable. For other policies (non-ULIP) issued after 1 April 2023, if aggregate annual premium exceeds ₹5 lakh, proceeds are taxable.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 10(10D); Finance Act 2021; Finance Act 2023

question: Is agricultural income taxable under the Income Tax Act?
response: Agricultural income, as defined under Section 2(1A), is fully exempt from income tax under Section 10(1). However, if a taxpayer also has non-agricultural income exceeding the basic exemption limit, agricultural income is included only for the purpose of determining the applicable tax rate (partial integration), not for actual taxation.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 10(1); Section 2(1A)

question: What is the tax exemption limit for gratuity for non-government employees?
response: For non-government employees covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972, gratuity is exempt under Section 10(10) up to ₹20,00,000. The exempt amount is the least of: actual gratuity received; 15 days' salary for each completed year of service; or ₹20,00,000.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 10(10); Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972; Notification No. SO 1213(E) 2019

question: What is the exemption limit for leave encashment for non-government employees?
response: Leave encashment received at retirement or resignation by non-government employees is exempt under Section 10(10AA) up to ₹25,00,000. The limit was revised from ₹3,00,000 to ₹25,00,000 effective FY 2023-24. The exempt amount is the least of: actual leave encashment; 10 months' average salary; cash equivalent of eligible leave (max 30 days per year); or ₹25,00,000.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 10(10AA); CBDT Notification 2023

question: Under which section is a minor child's income clubbed with the parent's income?
response: Under Section 64(1A), income earned by a minor child (below 18 years) is clubbed with the income of the parent whose income (before clubbing) is higher. An exemption of ₹1,500 per minor child is allowed. Income from manual work or skill/talent of the minor is not clubbed. Once income is clubbed in the hands of one parent, it continues to be clubbed with the same parent unless the Assessing Officer directs otherwise.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 64(1A)

question: How many days must an individual stay in India to qualify as a Resident for tax purposes?
response: Under Section 6(1), an individual is a Resident if: (a) present in India for 182 days or more during the previous year; or (b) present in India for 60 days or more during the previous year and 365 days or more in the preceding 4 years. For Indian citizens leaving for employment abroad, or citizens/PIOs visiting India, the 60-day condition is replaced by 182 days. From FY 2020-21, a citizen with income > ₹15 lakh and not taxable in any other country may be treated as deemed resident.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 6; Finance Act 2020

question: When is advance tax applicable and what are the installment due dates?
response: Advance tax is payable if estimated tax liability for the year exceeds ₹10,000 under Section 208. Due dates and percentages: 15th June (15%), 15th September (45%), 15th December (75%), 15th March (100%). Senior citizens not having business income are exempt from advance tax. Taxpayers under presumptive taxation (44AD/44ADA) must pay 100% advance tax by 15th March.
source: Income Tax Act, Sections 207, 208, 211

question: What interest is charged under Section 234B for default in advance tax payment?
response: Under Section 234B, interest at 1% per month (or part of month) is charged when: (a) advance tax paid is less than 90% of assessed tax; or (b) no advance tax was paid at all. Interest is calculated on the shortfall from 1st April of the assessment year until the date of actual tax payment or assessment.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 234B

question: What is interest under Section 234C for deferment of advance tax?
response: Under Section 234C, interest at 1% per month is charged for shortfall in each advance tax installment: (a) 3 months' interest if less than 12%/36%/75% paid by June/September/December; (b) 1 month's interest for shortfall in the March installment. This does not apply if no advance tax was paid — Section 234B covers that situation.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 234C

question: Under which section is TDS deducted on salary and who is responsible?
response: TDS on salary is deducted by the employer under Section 192 at the time of payment. The employer is required to deduct tax based on the employee's estimated total income for the year at the applicable average slab rate. The employee must declare their intended tax regime (New or Old) to the employer; otherwise, the employer deducts under the New Tax Regime by default.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 192; CBDT Circular 4/2023

question: Under which section is TDS deducted on interest other than securities, and what is the threshold?
response: TDS on interest (other than securities) is deducted under Section 194A at 10%. TDS is triggered when interest from a bank/cooperative bank/post office exceeds ₹40,000 in a year (₹50,000 for senior citizens). For other payers, the threshold is ₹5,000. If the payee does not furnish PAN, TDS is deducted at 20%.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 194A; Finance Act 2019

question: What is the holding period for long-term classification of listed equity shares?
response: For listed equity shares to be classified as long-term capital assets, the holding period must exceed 12 months. If held for 12 months or less, gains are treated as short-term. The date of acquisition is included but the date of transfer is excluded for calculating the holding period.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 2(42A)

question: What is the holding period for long-term classification of immovable property?
response: For immovable property (land, building, or both), the holding period must exceed 24 months to qualify as a long-term capital asset (reduced from 36 months effective FY 2017-18). Gains exceeding 24 months are taxed at 20% with indexation benefit under Section 112.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 2(42A); Finance Act 2017

question: What exemption is available under Section 54 on sale of residential property?
response: Under Section 54, an individual or HUF selling a residential house property can claim exemption on LTCG if the gains are invested in purchase of one residential house in India (within 1 year before or 2 years after the date of sale) or in construction of a residential house (within 3 years). The exemption is limited to the amount invested. From FY 2023-24, the exemption is capped at ₹10 crore.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 54; Finance Act 2023

question: What exemption is provided under Section 54F?
response: Under Section 54F, an individual or HUF selling any long-term capital asset other than a residential house can claim exemption if the entire net sale consideration (not just capital gains) is invested in a new residential property within the prescribed period. The exemption amount = Capital Gains × (Amount Invested / Net Consideration). Proportional exemption applies if partial investment is made. The taxpayer must not own more than one residential house (other than the new one) on the date of transfer.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 54F

question: What additional deduction is available under Section 80EEA for affordable housing loans?
response: Under Section 80EEA, a first-time home buyer can claim an additional deduction of up to ₹1,50,000 on interest paid on a home loan, over and above the ₹2,00,000 available under Section 24(b). Conditions: (a) loan sanctioned between 1 April 2019 and 31 March 2022; (b) stamp duty value of property does not exceed ₹45 lakh; (c) taxpayer should not own any other residential property on the date of sanction.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80EEA; Finance Act 2019

question: What is the additional NPS deduction available under Section 80CCD(1B)?
response: Under Section 80CCD(1B), an individual can claim an additional deduction of up to ₹50,000 for contributions made to the National Pension System (NPS) Tier-I account. This is over and above the ₹1,50,000 limit of Section 80C, making the combined maximum NPS-related deduction ₹2,00,000. This deduction is not available under the New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80CCD(1B); Finance Act 2015

question: Is employer contribution to NPS taxable, and what deduction is available?
response: Employer contribution to an employee's NPS Tier-I account is deductible under Section 80CCD(2): up to 10% of salary (basic + DA) for private sector employees, and up to 14% of salary for central government employees (enhanced from FY 2019-20). This deduction is available even under the New Tax Regime, making it one of the few deductions permitted under the new regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80CCD(2); Finance Act 2019

question: Are dividends received from Indian companies taxable in the hands of shareholders?
response: Yes. Post abolition of Dividend Distribution Tax (DDT) effective 1 April 2020, dividends are taxable in the hands of shareholders at applicable slab rates under the head 'Income from Other Sources'. TDS at 10% is deducted under Section 194 if dividend exceeds ₹5,000. For non-residents, TDS is at 20% (or lower DTAA rate). No deduction is allowed except interest expense up to 20% of dividend income.
source: Finance Act 2020; Income Tax Act, Sections 56, 194, 115-O

question: Is professional tax paid by an employee deductible from salary income?
response: Yes. Professional tax (also called employment tax) paid by an employee is fully deductible from gross salary income under Section 16(iii), without any monetary limit, before arriving at taxable income under the head 'Salaries'. This deduction is available under both Old and New Tax Regimes.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 16(iii)

question: Are gifts received from relatives taxable?
response: Gifts received from 'specified relatives' as defined under Section 56(2)(x) — such as spouse, siblings, siblings of spouse, lineal ascendants/descendants and their spouses — are fully exempt from tax regardless of amount and whether received in cash, cheque, or kind. Gifts received on the occasion of marriage are also fully exempt, regardless of the relationship.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 56(2)(x)

question: What is the exemption limit for gifts received from non-relatives?
response: Gifts received from non-relatives are taxable under Section 56(2)(x) as 'Income from Other Sources' if the aggregate value in a year exceeds ₹50,000. If the total value exceeds ₹50,000, the entire amount (not just the excess) is taxable. The rule applies to gifts in cash, cheque, jewellery, shares, or immovable property. Gifts received on the occasion of marriage or by will/inheritance are fully exempt.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 56(2)(x)

question: Under which section is depreciation allowed and how is it computed?
response: Depreciation on assets used for business or profession is allowed under Section 32 on a Written Down Value (WDV) basis at rates prescribed in Income Tax Rules (Appendix I). A 'block of assets' concept is used. For new plant and machinery (other than specified exclusions), an additional 20% depreciation (40% for certain vehicles and renewable energy equipment) is available in the year of acquisition if used for more than 180 days.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 32; Income Tax Rules, Appendix I

question: What deduction is available under Section 80GGA?
response: Under Section 80GGA, a 100% deduction is allowed for donations made to institutions engaged in scientific research or rural development, as approved under Section 35(1)(ii), 35(1)(iii), or Section 35AC. This deduction is available only to taxpayers who do not have income from business or profession. Donations must be made through non-cash modes.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80GGA

question: What deduction is available under Section 80GG for rent paid without HRA?
response: Under Section 80GG, an individual who does not receive HRA and pays rent for residential accommodation can claim deduction equal to the least of: (a) Rent paid minus 10% of total income; (b) ₹5,000 per month (₹60,000 annually); (c) 25% of total income. The taxpayer, spouse, or minor child should not own any residential property at the place of employment. Form 10BA must be filed. Not available under New Tax Regime.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 80GG; Rule 11B

question: Is PAN mandatory for filing an income tax return in India?
response: Yes. PAN (Permanent Account Number) is mandatory for filing income tax returns under Section 139A. If PAN is not quoted, the return is treated as defective. Aadhaar can be used as an alternative to PAN in certain cases. Additionally, PAN-Aadhaar linking is mandatory to keep PAN operative.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 139A; Section 139AA

question: What is the Annual Information Statement (AIS) and what does it contain?
response: Annual Information Statement (AIS) is a comprehensive document available on the income tax portal under Section 285BB showing all financial transactions reported to the Income Tax Department. It includes: salary, interest, dividends, mutual fund/stock transactions, GST turnover, foreign remittances, purchase/sale of property, and more. Taxpayers can provide feedback on incorrect or duplicate information. It is broader than Form 26AS and is mandatory to review before filing returns.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 285BB; CBDT Notification 2021

question: What is Form 26AS and how does it differ from AIS?
response: Form 26AS is a consolidated annual tax credit statement available on the TRACES portal under Section 203AA. It shows TDS, TCS, advance tax, and self-assessment tax paid against the taxpayer's PAN. Unlike AIS, it does not cover all financial transactions. AIS is more comprehensive and effectively replaces Form 26AS for reconciliation purposes, though Form 26AS is still issued officially.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 203AA; Income Tax Rules, Rule 31AB

question: When is an income tax refund issued and how is it processed?
response: An income tax refund arises under Section 237 when taxes paid (TDS + TCS + advance tax + self-assessment tax) exceed the actual tax liability after processing. The refund, along with interest at 0.5% per month under Section 244A (from 1 April or date of payment to date of grant), is credited directly to the taxpayer's validated bank account. CBDT processes refunds after return verification and CPC (Centralized Processing Centre) intimation under Section 143(1).
source: Income Tax Act, Sections 237, 244A, 143(1)

question: Can a belated return be filed after the due date?
response: Yes. A belated return under Section 139(4) can be filed after the due date but before 31st December of the relevant assessment year (earlier it was 31st March, revised by Finance Act 2021). A belated return attracts: (a) late filing fee under Section 234F; (b) interest under Sections 234A, 234B, 234C; and (c) loss of right to carry forward most losses (except house property losses).
source: Income Tax Act, Section 139(4); Finance Act 2021

question: What is an Updated Return under Section 139(8A)?
response: An Updated Return ('ITR-U') under Section 139(8A) allows any taxpayer to file a return or correct errors in a previously filed return within 24 months from the end of the relevant assessment year. An additional tax of 25% of tax + interest is payable if filed within 12 months, and 50% if filed after 12 months but within 24 months. It cannot be used to claim a refund or reduce tax liability.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 139(8A); Finance Act 2022

question: Can business losses be carried forward and for how many years?
response: Under Section 72, losses from business or profession (other than speculative business) can be carried forward for a maximum of 8 assessment years to be set off against income from any business or profession. The return of loss must be filed on time. Speculative business losses can only be set off against speculative income (Section 73) and can be carried forward for 4 years.
source: Income Tax Act, Sections 72, 73

question: Can loss from house property be set off against other income?
response: Yes. Under Section 71, loss from house property can be set off against income from any other head (salary, business, capital gains, etc.) but is capped at ₹2,00,000 per year. The unabsorbed loss beyond ₹2,00,000 can be carried forward for 8 assessment years and set off only against house property income. This set-off of ₹2 lakh is available under the Old Tax Regime only.
source: Income Tax Act, Sections 71, 71B

question: What is Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) and who does it apply to?
response: Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) under Section 115JC applies to non-corporate taxpayers (individuals, HUFs, AOPs, BOIs, LLPs, firms) claiming certain profit-linked deductions (such as Section 10AA or Chapter VI-A deductions under heading 'C — Deductions in respect of certain incomes'). AMT is levied at 18.5% (+surcharge+cess) on adjusted total income. If normal tax < AMT, the taxpayer pays AMT and can carry forward AMT credit under Section 115JD for up to 15 years.
source: Income Tax Act, Sections 115JC, 115JD

question: What is Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) and what is its rate for companies?
response: Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) under Section 115JB ensures that companies with large profits and zero/low tax liability (due to exemptions/deductions) pay a minimum tax. MAT is levied at 15% of book profits (as per Companies Act) plus applicable surcharge and cess. Domestic companies opting for the concessional 22% tax rate under Section 115BAA or 115BAB are exempt from MAT.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 115JB; Finance Act 2019

question: What is the basic exemption limit for senior citizens under the Old Tax Regime?
response: Senior citizens aged 60 years or above but below 80 years have a basic exemption limit of ₹3,00,000 under the Old Tax Regime. Super senior citizens (80 years and above) have an exemption limit of ₹5,00,000. Under the New Tax Regime, the exemption limit is ₹3,00,000 for all individuals irrespective of age.
source: Income Tax Act, First Schedule; Finance Act 2023

question: What are the special tax provisions for super senior citizens?
response: Super senior citizens (aged 80 years and above) have a basic exemption of ₹5,00,000 under the Old Regime. They are not required to pay advance tax if no business/profession income exists. They can file ITR in paper form. TDS is not deducted on FD interest up to ₹50,000 (Section 194A). They are also not eligible for Section 87A rebate as their income generally exceeds the rebate threshold.
source: Income Tax Act, First Schedule; Sections 207, 194A

question: Is agricultural income considered for rate purposes even if exempt?
response: Yes. Agricultural income, though exempt under Section 10(1), is included for rate purposes under the partial integration method when the taxpayer is an individual, HUF, AOP, BOI, or artificial juridical person. Tax is computed on non-agricultural income + agricultural income; then tax on agricultural income + basic exemption limit is deducted. The difference is the actual tax payable.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 10(1); Sections 2 and 5 of Finance Acts

question: What constitutes perquisites and how are they taxed?
response: Perquisites under Section 17(2) are benefits provided by the employer to the employee or their family members over and above salary. Examples: rent-free accommodation, company car, ESOP, club membership, LTC, interest-free loans. Perquisite value is computed per Rule 3 and added to salary income. Some perquisites are taxable in all cases; others are taxable only for specified employees (whose monetary salary exceeds ₹50,000 per month).
source: Income Tax Act, Section 17(2); Income Tax Rules, Rule 3

question: What is the standard deduction allowed on house property income?
response: Under Section 24(a), a standard deduction of 30% of the Net Annual Value (NAV) is allowed as a flat deduction from income from house property to cover repairs, maintenance, and other expenses. No supporting bills or documents are required. This deduction applies to let-out or deemed let-out properties. For self-occupied property, NAV is nil, so this deduction has no practical impact.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 24(a)

question: Can long-term capital loss be set off against short-term capital gains?
response: No. Under Section 74, long-term capital loss (LTCL) can only be set off against long-term capital gains (LTCG), not against short-term capital gains. However, short-term capital loss (STCL) can be set off against both short-term and long-term capital gains. Unabsorbed capital losses (both LTCL and STCL) can be carried forward for 8 assessment years.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 74; Section 70

question: What is the TDS rate on dividend income from equity shares?
response: TDS on dividend from domestic companies is deducted under Section 194 at 10% if dividend income exceeds ₹5,000 in a financial year per company. For non-resident shareholders, TDS is under Section 195/196D at 20% plus surcharge and cess (subject to DTAA benefits). TDS is deducted at the time of payment or credit of dividend, whichever is earlier.
source: Income Tax Act, Sections 194, 196D; Finance Act 2020

question: At what rate are lottery winnings, game show prizes, or horse race winnings taxed?
response: Under Section 115BB, income from winnings in lotteries, crossword puzzles, card games, gambling, betting, or horse races is taxed at a flat rate of 30% (plus applicable surcharge and 4% cess), regardless of the taxpayer's total income or slab. No deductions or exemptions are allowed from such income. TDS is deducted at 30% under Section 194B/194BB if winnings exceed ₹10,000 in a transaction.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 115BB; Sections 194B, 194BB

question: What is the tax treatment of income from Virtual Digital Assets (VDAs) such as crypto?
response: Under Section 115BBH introduced in Finance Act 2022, income from transfer of Virtual Digital Assets (including cryptocurrency and NFTs) is taxed at 30% plus surcharge and 4% cess. No deductions are allowed except the cost of acquisition. Losses from VDA cannot be set off against any other income or carried forward. TDS at 1% under Section 194S applies to VDA transactions above the prescribed threshold (₹50,000 or ₹10,000 depending on the payer type).
source: Income Tax Act, Section 115BBH; Section 194S; Finance Act 2022

question: What is TCS on foreign remittance under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS)?
response: Under Section 206C(1G), Tax Collected at Source (TCS) applies on foreign remittances under LRS: 20% on all remittances exceeding ₹7 lakh per year (except education/medical remittances where the rate is 5%). Remittances for education financed by loan attract TCS at 0.5% above ₹7 lakh threshold. TCS paid can be claimed as credit against final tax liability or refunded. Effective 1 October 2023 as per Finance Act 2023.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 206C(1G); Finance Act 2023; CBDT Circular 10/2023

question: When is a tax audit mandatory under Section 44AB?
response: Tax audit under Section 44AB is mandatory when: (a) Business turnover exceeds ₹1 crore (₹10 crore if 95%+ receipts and payments are digital); (b) Professional gross receipts exceed ₹50 lakh; (c) A person opts out of presumptive taxation (44AD/44ADA) or declares income below presumptive rates. The audit must be conducted by a Chartered Accountant and the report filed in Form 3CA/3CB and 3CD by 30th September of the assessment year.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 44AB; Finance Act 2021

question: Is linking PAN with Aadhaar mandatory and what are the consequences of non-linking?
response: Yes. Under Section 139AA, quoting Aadhaar in the income tax return and linking PAN with Aadhaar is mandatory for resident individuals eligible to obtain Aadhaar. Failure to link by 31 May 2024 renders PAN inoperative. An inoperative PAN results in: TDS/TCS at higher rates (Section 206AA/206CC), inability to open bank accounts, demat accounts, or carry out high-value transactions. A late linking fee of ₹1,000 is payable. PAN becomes operative again once linked.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 139AA; Finance Act 2017; CBDT Circular 3/2023

question: What is CPC in income tax and what is its role?
response: CPC stands for Centralized Processing Centre, located in Bengaluru. It processes income tax returns filed electronically under Section 143(1) without any interface with the taxpayer. CPC issues intimations under Section 143(1) after processing — confirming the tax return as filed, or identifying discrepancies between reported income and data available (TDS mismatch, etc.). Refunds are also processed and credited by CPC. CPC is managed by the Income Tax Department and operated through TRACES/e-filing portal.
source: Income Tax Act, Section 143(1); Income Tax Department, CPC Bengaluru
